infobox.key: Ordnance Survey National Grid reference system
infobox.value: (**OSGB**), also known as **British National Grid** (**BNG**),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a system of geographic [grid references](/wiki/Grid_reference "Grid reference"), distinct from [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), whereby any location in Great Britain can be described in terms of its distance from the origin (0, 0), which lies to the west of the [Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Isles_of_Scilly "Isles of Scilly").[[3]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

System of geographic grid references used in Great Britain

"British National Grid" redirects here. For the electricity network, see [National Grid (Great Britain)](/wiki/National_Grid_(Great_Britain) "National Grid (Great Britain)").

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Geodesy "Template:Geodesy") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Geodesy "Template talk:Geodesy") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy "Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy") |

The **Ordnance Survey National Grid reference system** (**OSGB**), also known as **British National Grid** (**BNG**),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a system of geographic [grid references](/wiki/Grid_reference "Grid reference"), distinct from [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), whereby any location in Great Britain can be described in terms of its distance from the origin (0, 0), which lies to the west of the [Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Isles_of_Scilly "Isles of Scilly").[[3]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-3)

Devised by the [Ordnance Survey](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey "Ordnance Survey") (OS), the national grid reference system is heavily used in OS survey data and in maps based on those surveys, whether published by the Ordnance Survey or by commercial map producers. Grid references are also commonly quoted in other publications and data sources, such as guide books and government planning documents.

A number of different systems exist that can provide grid references for locations within the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"): this article describes the system created solely for Great Britain and its outlying islands (including the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man")). The [Irish grid reference system](/wiki/Irish_grid_reference_system "Irish grid reference system") is a similar system created by the [Ordnance Survey of Ireland](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_of_Ireland "Ordnance Survey of Ireland") and the [Ordnance Survey of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_of_Northern_Ireland "Ordnance Survey of Northern Ireland") for the island of Ireland. The [Irish Transverse Mercator](/wiki/Irish_Transverse_Mercator "Irish Transverse Mercator") (ITM) coordinate reference system was adopted in 2001 and is now the preferred coordinate reference system across Ireland. ITM is based on the [Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator_coordinate_system "Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system") (UTM), used to provide grid references for worldwide locations, and this is the system commonly used for the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands"). European-wide agencies also use UTM when mapping locations, or may use the [Military Grid Reference System](/wiki/Military_Grid_Reference_System "Military Grid Reference System") (MGRS), or variants of it.

## Grid letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Grid letters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/British_National_Grid.svg/250px-British_National_Grid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:British_National_Grid.svg)

100km squares

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Ordnance_Survey_1-250000_-_TF.jpg/250px-Ordnance_Survey_1-250000_-_TF.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ordnance_Survey_1-250000_-_TF.jpg)

Grid square TF. The map shows [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), as well as places within the counties of [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"), Cambridgeshire and [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk").

The first letter of the British National Grid is derived from a larger set of 25 squares of size 500 km by 500 km, labelled A to Z, omitting one letter (I) (refer diagram below), previously used as a military grid.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Four of these largest squares contain significant land area within Great Britain: S, T, N and H. The O square contains a tiny area of [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire"), [Beast Cliff](/wiki/Beast_Cliff "Beast Cliff") at [OV 0000](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&params=54.385675_N_0.461660_W_region:GB_scale:25000), almost all of which lies below mean high tide.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
For the second letter, each 500 km square is subdivided into 25 squares of size 100 km by 100 km, each with a letter code from A to Z (again omitting I) starting with A in the north-west corner to Z in the south-east corner. These squares are outlined in light grey on the "100km squares" map, with those containing land lettered. The central (2° W) meridian is shown in red.

## Grid digits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Grid digits")]

Within each square, *eastings* and *northings* from the south west corner of the square are given numerically. For example, NH0325 means a 1 km square whose south-west corner is 3 km east and 25 km north from the south-west corner of square NH. A location can be indicated to varying resolutions numerically, usually from two digits in each coordinate (for a 1 km square) through to five (for a 1 m square); in each case the first half of the digits is for the first coordinate and the second half for the other. The most common usage is the *six figure grid reference*, employing three digits in each coordinate to determine a 100 m square. For example, the grid reference of the 100 m square containing the summit of [Ben Nevis](/wiki/Ben_Nevis "Ben Nevis") is **[NN 166 712](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&params=56.796029_N_5.004711_W_region:GB_scale:25000&title=NN+166+712)**. (Grid references may be written with or without spaces; e.g., also NN166712.) NN has an easting of 200 km and northing of 700 km, so the OSGB36 National Grid location for Ben Nevis is at 216600, 771200.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid.svg/500px-Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid.svg)

Illustration of the Ordnance Survey National Grid coordinate system, with [Royal Observatory Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory_Greenwich "Royal Observatory Greenwich") as an example

## All-numeric grid references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: All-numeric grid references")]

Grid references may also be quoted as a pair of numbers: eastings then northings in metres, measured from the south-west corner of the SV square. Thirteen digits may be required for locations in [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") and further north. For example, the grid reference for [Sullom Voe Oil Terminal](/wiki/Sullom_Voe_Oil_Terminal "Sullom Voe Oil Terminal") in the [Shetland Islands](/wiki/Shetland_Islands "Shetland Islands") may be given as [HU396753](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&params=60.459520_N_1.281867_W_region:GB_scale:25000&title=HU396753) or 439668,1175316.

Another, distinct, form of all-numeric grid reference is an abbreviated alphanumeric reference where the letters are simply omitted, e.g. 166712 for the summit of Ben Nevis. Unlike the numeric references described above, this abbreviated grid reference is incomplete; it gives the location relative to an OS 100×100 km square, but does not specify which square. It is often used informally when the context identifies the OS 2-letter square. For example, within the context of a location known to be on [OS Landranger sheet](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey#Map_range "Ordnance Survey") 41 (which extends from NN000500 in the south-west to NN400900 in the north-east) the abbreviated grid reference 166712 is equivalent to NN166712. If working with more than one Landranger sheet, this may also be given as 41/166712.

Alternatively, numbers are sometimes used instead of the two-letter combinations for the 100×100 km squares. The numbering follows a grid index where the tens denote the progress from West to East and the units from South to North. In the north of Scotland, the numbering is modified: the 100 km square to the north of 39 is numbered N30; the square to the north of 49 is N40, etc.

## Compatibility with related systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Compatibility with related systems")]

The grid is based on the **OSGB36 datum** (Ordnance Survey Great Britain 1936). The [datum](/wiki/Map_datum "Map datum") was introduced after the [retriangulation of 1936–1962](/wiki/Retriangulation_of_Great_Britain "Retriangulation of Great Britain").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It replaced the [Cassini Grid](/wiki/Cassini_Grid "Cassini Grid") which had previously been the standard projection for Ordnance Survey maps.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
A [datum transformation](/wiki/Datum_transformation "Datum transformation") exists between GRS36 and more recent geocentric frames (see below).

OSGB36 is based on the **Airy ellipsoid**, a [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid") named after [George Biddell Airy](/wiki/George_Biddell_Airy "George Biddell Airy"). Introduced in 1830, it is a best fit for the Britain region. More modern mapping tends to use the [GRS80](/wiki/GRS80 "GRS80") or [WGS 84](/wiki/WGS_84 "WGS 84") ellipsoid, as used by the [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System"). The Airy ellipsoid assumes the Earth to be about 1 km smaller in diameter than the global/world ellipsoid, and to be slightly less flattened.

The British maps adopt a [transverse Mercator projection](/wiki/Transverse_Mercator_projection "Transverse Mercator projection") with an origin (the "true" origin) at [49° N](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north"), [2° W](/wiki/2nd_meridian_west "2nd meridian west") (an offshore point in the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") which lies between the island of [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") and the French port of [St. Malo](/wiki/St._Malo "St. Malo")).[[8]](#cite_note-8) Over the Airy ellipsoid a straight line grid, the National Grid, is placed with a new [false origin](/wiki/False_origin "False origin") to eliminate negative numbers, creating a 700 km by 1300 km grid. This false origin is located south-west of the Isles of Scilly.

In order to minimize the overall scale error, a factor of 2499/2500 is applied. This creates two secant lines of longitude about 180 km east and west of the central meridian along which the local scale factor equals 1, i.e. map scale is correct. Inside these lines the local scale factor is less than 1, with a minimum of 0.04% too small at the central meridian.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Outside these lines the local scale factor is greater than 1, and is about 0.04% too large near the east and west coasts. Grid north and true north are only aligned on the central meridian (400 km easting) of the grid which is 2° W (OSGB36) and approx. 2° 0′ 5″ W ([WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System")).

A [geodetic](/wiki/Geodetic "Geodetic") transformation between OSGB36 and other terrestrial reference systems (like [ITRF2000](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System "International Terrestrial Reference System"), [ETRS89](/wiki/ETRS89 "ETRS89"), or [WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System")) can become quite tedious if attempted manually. The most common transformation is called the [Helmert datum transformation](/wiki/Helmert_transformation "Helmert transformation"), which results in a typical 7 m error. The definitive transformation from ETRS89 that is published by the Ordnance Survey is called the National Grid Transformation OSTN15.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) This models the detailed distortions in the 1936–1962 retriangulation, and achieves backwards compatibility in grid coordinates to sub-metre accuracy.

### Datum shift between OSGB 36 and WGS 84

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Datum shift between OSGB 36 and WGS 84")]

The difference between the coordinates on different datums varies from place to place. The [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") and [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") positions on OSGB 36 are the same as for [WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") at a point in the Atlantic Ocean well to the west of Great Britain. In [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), the WGS 84 longitude meridians are about 70 metres east of their OSGB 36 equivalents, this value rising gradually to about 120 m east on the east coast of [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"). The WGS 84 latitude parallels are about 70 m south of the OSGB 36 lines in South Cornwall, the difference diminishing to zero in the [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders"), and then increasing to about 50 m north on the north coast of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). The smallest datum shift is on the west coast of Scotland and the greatest in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent").

### Datum shift between OSGB 36 and ED50

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Datum shift between OSGB 36 and ED50")]

These two datums are not both in general use in any one place, but for a point in the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") halfway between [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover") and [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais"), the [ED50](/wiki/ED50 "ED50") longitude lines are about 20 m east of the OSGB36 equivalents, and the ED50 latitude lines are about 150 m south of the OSGB36 ones.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Summary parameters of the coordinate system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Summary parameters of the coordinate system")]

* [Datum](/wiki/Datum_(geodesy) "Datum (geodesy)"): OSGB36
* [Map projection](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection"): [Transverse Mercator projection](/wiki/Transverse_Mercator_projection "Transverse Mercator projection") using [Redfearn series](/wiki/Transverse_Mercator:_Redfearn_series "Transverse Mercator: Redfearn series")
* True origin: [49° N](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north"), [2° W](/wiki/2nd_meridian_west "2nd meridian west")
* [False origin](/wiki/False_origin "False origin"): 400 km west, 100 km north of True Origin
* Scale factor: 0.9996012717[[a]](#cite_note-13)
* [EPSG](/wiki/EPSG "EPSG") Code: EPSG:27700
* [Ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"): Airy 1830[[b]](#cite_note-15)
* [Semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") a: 6377563.396 m
* [Semi-minor axis](/wiki/Semi-minor_axis "Semi-minor axis") b: 6356256.909 m
* [Flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") (derived constant): 1/299.3249646

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** Scale factor is defined by its base-10 logarithm of (0.9998268 − 1) exactly.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** The defining Airy dimensions are a 20923713 feet, b 20853810 feet. In the Retriangulation the base-10 logarithm of the number of metres in a foot was set at (0.48401603 − 1)[[13]](#cite_note-14) exactly and the Airy metric dimensions are calculated from that. The flattening is exactly 69903 divided by 20923713.
